min_doctor_qualification: MS/MD/ DNB/ equivalent (Ophthalmology) with specialist training in Retina/ ROP
clinical_indications: Infants meeting screening criteria: Gestation < 34 weeks or Birth weight < 2000 grams, or any preterm infant with risk factors (Cardiorespiratory support, Chronic lung disease, Exchange transfusion, Poor post-natal weight gain, Prolonged oxygen requirement, Blood transfusion, Intraventricular haemorrhage, Respiratory distress syndrome, Sepsis, Apnea)
ROP classification: Zone 1, Stage 1 (with or without Plus disease)
ROP classification: Zone 1, Stage 2 (with or without Plus disease)
ROP classification: Zone 1, Stage 3 (with or without Plus disease)
ROP classification: Zone 2, Stage 1 (with Plus disease)
ROP classification: Zone 2, Stage 2 (with Plus disease)
Laser treatment to be done according to ET-ROP (Early Treatment for ROP) guidelines
Laser treatment to be applied within 48 hours of diagnosis
Diagnosis of aggressive posterior ROP (APROP) requiring laser as soon as possible

clinical_thresholds.note: Treatment indicated for ROP in Zone 1, Stage 1
Treatment indicated for ROP in Zone 1, Stage 2
Treatment indicated for ROP in Zone 1, Stage 3
Treatment indicated for ROP in Zone 2, Stage 1 with Plus disease
Treatment indicated for ROP in Zone 2, Stage 2 with Plus disease

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes and Ophthalmoscopy reports / stills showing extent of ROP and need for procedure.
Was the neonateborn at less than 34 weeks of gestation/ low birth weight <2000 grams / any risk factor as mentioned above)?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Are the detailed laser procedure / Operative Notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?
Was the indication of surgery documented?

common_queries: Indirect ophthalmoscopy report not suggestive of diagnosis or not indicative of need for procedure/surgery.
Clinical notes and Ophthalmoscopy reports/stills missing or not adequately showing extent of ROP and need for procedure.
Neonate not born at less than 34 weeks of gestation, or not low birth weight (<2000 grams), or without documented risk factors for ROP.
Detailed laser procedure / Operative Notes missing or incomplete.
Indication of surgery not documented for the procedure.

additional_information.special_empanelment: Care at District/Tertiary Hospital (SNCU/NICU), Eye hospital/SNCU-NICU/District Early Intervention Centre (DEIC) for follow up
clinical_key_pointers: Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP) is a retinal disorder of low birth weight premature infants.
ROP can be mild with no visual defects, or it may become aggressive with new vessel formation (neo-vascularisation) and progress to retinal detachment and blindness.
The stimulus for the abnormal growth of blood vessels comes from the peripheral immature retina.
International Classification of ROP (ICROP) is used for classifying ROP.

ROP is categorised based on: Severity of the disease into stages (0-5), Location of the disease into 3 zones (Zone 1-3), Extent of the disease based on clock hours (1-12) and the presence of plus disease.
Initial screen for infants at 4 weeks (30 days) of birth. For infants with period of gestation less than 28 weeks or weighing less than 1200 gms, first screening to be done at 2-3 weeks after birth.

Recommended follow up intervals for infants at risk: 2-3 week intervals until retina is fully vascularised if no signs of ROP. Specific intervals (e.g. 1-2 weeks, 1 week or less, 2-3 weeks) for different zones and stages if ROP is present.
surgical_options: Laser therapy: Principle is to remove the stimulus for growth of abnormal new blood vessels by ablating the peripheral avascular retina to reduce retinal detachment and blindness. This abolishes hypoxic drive (mediated by VEGF) leading to regression of established ROP.

Treatment of advanced disease: Laser photocoagulation is not successful in all cases. Such cases should be referred to centers dealing with advanced retinal surgery. Complex vitreoretinal surgery by an expert can prevent blindness once retinal detachment has occurred.
